source: Synthesised from @padeldriver YouTube technique videos (transcripts). Cues/mistakes are paraphrased coaching criteria, not verbatim transcript. Powers the labeling tool's technique_flags and the Player Score technique pillar (scoring_model.json).
status: 7 of 9 shot-groups; drives + bajada/rulo/chiquita/slice pending agent completion

shots.purpose: A defensive overhead played off a lob to neutralize it, keep the ball deep, and hold your net position rather than to win the point outright.
cues: Treat it as a defensive positional shot: tap the ball back deep and reclaim the net rather than swinging for a winner.
The instant you read the lob, turn sideways and take the racket back while you set your feet.
Keep the non-hitting arm up and pointing at the ball to align the shoulders and coil the body.
Contact out in front and to the side at roughly eye height, not directly overhead or behind the head.
Get behind the ball early, load the back foot, then transfer forward through contact and finish moving to the net.
Let the ball drop to eye level instead of hitting it high and steep (which gives a high rebound off the glass).
Slice it: bring the racket slightly from above and across so it skids low off the back glass and dies.
Rotate the whole chain (hips, shoulders, arm) and stay relaxed rather than muscling with the wrist.
Aim deep behind the service line, ideally cross-court so it catches back then side glass and displaces the opponent.
Mix targets (cross, centre, occasional down-the-line) and keep the centre rebound extra low.
mistakes: Playing it too aggressively/fast, risking errors and leaving no time to recover the net.
Hitting high-to-low too steeply or too weakly, so it lands short and bounces up as an easy counter.
Staying back and admiring it instead of finishing with weight forward toward the net.
Falling backward at contact instead of transferring weight forward.
Playing it while still moving rather than being set as the opponent strikes.
Facing the net chest-square instead of turning sideways with the left hand.
Over-using the wrist for power instead of arm and body rotation.
Producing a high rebound (especially to the centre) that hands an easy counter.

phases.preparation: From volley-ready with racket tip up in the non-dominant hand; on reading the lob turn sideways, prepare the racket and set the feet, moving back with side steps.
phases.backswing: Weight loads onto the back foot, racket up and slightly above the intended contact, non-hitting arm raised at the ball, body coiled sideways.
phases.contact: Strike out in front and to the side at eye height with a slight downward-and-across slice, relaxed, sending it deep with a low skidding rebound.
phases.follow_through: Push through the ball and extend forward (hand finishing over the net), transferring weight forward so you are already advancing to the net.

shots.purpose: An aggressive attacking overhead off a shortish lob to add side-spin, pressure the opponents, block their net rush, and go for the winner rather than just reset.
cues: Use a continental grip, or drop slightly toward a backhand grip to load extra side-spin.
On reading the lob, turn sideways and set feet and racket together, shoulders up, racket raised; prepare a touch longer than a bandeja.
Contact at eye height and out to your side, letting the ball travel a bit behind you so full rotation fires.
Brush the OUTSIDE of the ball at the 3 o'clock point for side-spin; keep a loose, unlocked wrist for the snap.
Rotate hips-then-shoulders-then-wrist together and stack that rotation into the ball for spin and head speed.
Come from above and slightly down-and-across so it drops fast and stays low after the glass (not up-and-out).
Stay compact — you don't need full arm extension; let rotation supply the speed for margin.
Aim mainly cross-court (more margin), mix down-the-line into the right-side player's body; vary pace, angle and spin.
mistakes: Taking it too high, sending it into the glass and kicking it up for an easy rebound.
Not rotating chest and feet enough, so the contact point sits wrong.
Hitting too early and too far in front instead of letting it drop to eye height beside/behind you.
Reaching far from the body with a fully extended arm, draining power and opening the face so it flies out.
Preparing the racket below/level with the ball so the swing pushes up-and-out instead of from above.
Rushing under nerves, raising contact height and killing the low spinny trajectory.
Playing every víbora flat-out with no variation, so opponents adapt.
Brushing over the top (6 o'clock, bandeja-style) instead of the outside 3 o'clock, losing the side-spin.

phases.preparation: Read the short lob early, turn sideways with shoulders up, set feet and racket together (feet almost to the target corner); prepare a bit longer/higher than a bandeja.
phases.backswing: Racket raised near/above head height, tip to the ceiling, so you can come down onto the outside of the ball; wait for it to drop.
phases.contact: Strike at eye height and slightly to the side/behind, brushing the OUTSIDE (3 o'clock) with a loose wrist snap while the body unwinds — from above, for side-spin that drives it down and low.
phases.follow_through: Continue across and down like a bandeja, finishing to the opposite side, so the ball stays low and kicks wide off the glass into the corner.

shots.purpose: Played at the net to keep and press the attacking position — an aggressive point-ender or a firm controlled block depending on the incoming ball.
cues: Continental grip, held loosely as if pointing at the ball with the face.
Ready between the posts: knees bent and active, racket up at chest height, tip up.
Non-dominant hand cradles the throat so the frame is supported until you turn.
Rotate the shoulders to prepare rather than pulling the arm straight back.
Keep the take-back short and compact for fast balls; only lengthen on a slow ball you choose to attack.
Contact out in front of the chest, not late beside the body.
Transfer weight and step toward the target at contact (down-the-line vs cross changes the step angle).
Punch/block through the ball; don't swing or slap with the wrist.
Slice by preparing slightly above the ball with a flatter, slightly open face rather than breaking the wrist.
Push forward through contact for length instead of chopping down; keep the ball low after the bounce.
mistakes: Over-using/breaking the wrist to slice instead of face angle and shoulder turn.
Long exaggerated backswing that leaves you late on fast balls.
Letting the non-dominant hand drop instead of keeping it up on the racket.
Contacting too late or beside the body instead of in front.
Following through steeply to the ground, sending the ball too high.
Aiming too deep near the back glass, giving an easy predictable ball off the wall.
No weight transfer / no step-in, losing power and balance.
Failing to recover to ready after the shot.

phases.preparation: Ready between the posts, knees bent and active, racket up at chest height tip up, left hand on the throat; react as the ball comes.
phases.backswing: Short compact take-back driven by shoulder rotation, not the arm; lengthen only for a chosen attacking ball.
phases.contact: Meet the ball out in front of the chest with a firm punch/block, face slightly open for slice, stepping toward the target as you strike.
phases.follow_through: Compact push forward for length (not a downward chop); keep it low, then step back to ready.

shots.purpose: Net shot on the backhand side — critical for a right-hander on the right court because most balls funnel to the centre backhand; aggressive for a winner or a controlled block. Power must come from the body, not just the arm.
cues: Continental grip; verify the grip before the rest of the motion.
Ready at chest height but a step back off the net so you have time to step in.
Prepare the racket up toward left-ear height (above the ball) so slice comes naturally, not from the wrist.
Keep prep short and compact on fast balls; lengthen only on slow high balls you choose to attack.
Rotate the shoulders and leave the ball to one side; never hit chest-square.
Actively use the non-dominant hand: grip the throat, then pull it back as you hit to fire both sides.
Step in with the front foot at contact to transfer weight and take time from the opponent.
Take the ball in front and while still high.
Finish compact and stopped; on cross/middle you don't need to over-rotate.
mistakes: Dead non-dominant hand: hitting with only the dominant side, killing power and balance.
Standing too close to the net, crowding the ball and finishing into the net.
Racket too far back, arriving late and losing impact on firm balls.
Contacting too late instead of in front.
Loading the back leg but never stepping in with the front, so stored power is unused.
Chest at the opponent with no shoulder rotation, making a fast backhand impossible.
Aiming too deep near the glass, giving an easy ball off the wall or inviting a lob.
Over-rotating on a cross/middle ball that needs little rotation.
Not recovering to ready after the shot.

phases.preparation: Ready at chest height a step off the net, continental grip, left hand on the throat; read the ball, decide attack vs block.
phases.backswing: Short compact take-back with the racket lifted toward left-ear height (above the ball) via shoulder rotation; lengthen only for a slow high ball.
phases.contact: Rotate the shoulders and step in with the front foot at impact, meeting the ball in front and still high; pull back with the non-dominant hand to fire both sides.
phases.follow_through: Short explosive stopped finish (arm stops, not fully extended), non-dominant hand pulled back for balance; eyes on the ball, then step back to recover.

shots.purpose: An attacking overhead off a short lob to finish the point or force a weak reply — flat and down for pace, or brushed up-and-out over the side glass (kick smash) to bounce it out of the court.
cues: Get the racket up and loaded early — take it back the instant you read the lob so the elbow points at the sky.
Turn side-on and point/track the ball with the non-racket arm; stay behind the ball so it stays in front, not behind the head.
Move the feet fast to get fully underneath the ball; adjust with small steps rather than planting flat.
Contact at the highest point you can reach, above and slightly in front of the head; pros choke down slightly for reach.
Drive from the ground up — load the legs, lean back, then extend into the ball; power comes from legs, not arm.
Keep the wrist relaxed through the load, then snap it at contact for the final acceleration.
Flat power smash: aim down-the-line (less court to cross), hit through the ball for a high back-glass bounce.
Kick smash (por tres): go extra sideways, brush up the back of the ball for topspin, strike the back-glass corner ~3m high so it kicks up and out — hit up-and-out, not down.
Choose by lob quality and conditions: more topspin/less power in wind or outdoors.
Play a high-percentage smash you can repeat ~70% of the time; if smashes keep failing, switch to bandeja.
mistakes: Late preparation — arriving with the racket low and lifting only as the ball drops, so contact is rushed and weak.
Letting the ball get behind the head — not getting underneath, striking behind you.
Hitting too low (head height like a víbora) instead of full reach, killing the bounce.
'Arming' the ball / no leg drive — no bend-and-extend, so no real power.
Over-using the wrist on the kick smash while body-blocked instead of full footwork/rotation/legs.
Facing the net square with no shoulder rotation, losing body-weight transfer.
Trying to kill every ball / pace over control, breaking timing (worse in wind).
Wrong grip for the kick (eastern forehand) so the ball can't lift and go out.
Aiming the kick bounce too short/early so opponents step in before the glass.

phases.preparation: Read the lob early, turn side-on, take the racket straight up so the elbow points at the sky; move the feet fast to get fully underneath and point at the ball with the non-racket hand.
phases.backswing: Loaded 'trophy' position — racket back and high behind the head, chest and free arm up, legs bent and weight leaning back like a spring, wrist relaxed.
phases.contact: Extend the legs up into the ball and strike at the highest reachable point above and slightly in front of the head; flat = punch down for pace, kick = brush up the back toward the back-glass corner.
phases.follow_through: Rotate the core through for weight transfer and snap the wrist; flat = drive down-and-through for a high glass bounce, kick = stay compact and downward then recover.

shots.purpose: An underarm below-the-waist drop-and-hit whose job is to start the point with an advantage and get to the net first, not to hit an outright winner.
cues: Drop/gently push the ball from ~shoulder height so contact happens right at the waistline (flatter, faster path).
Prepare the racket ABOVE the ball and swing high-to-low to slice; slice makes it die and stay low off the side glass.
At impact open the face ~45 degrees, then push forward through the ball for slice AND length (don't chop straight down).
Prioritise heavy slice over flat pace; a fast flat serve returns quick and leaves no time to reach the net.
Choose direction with the contact point, not the wrist: ball more in front for the T, further back to angle into the side glass.
Aim into the side glass, at the T, or straight at the body (the body serve jams them).
Control length: deep near the line so they can't step in; the T a touch shorter so it dies.
Keep the ball-drop hand in tension after release for balance.
Transfer weight forward into the hit and let momentum carry you into the net.
Stay unpredictable: vary direction, length, slice and pace, and remember what beat each opponent.
mistakes: Tossing the ball up like tennis instead of a controlled drop-and-hit.
Contacting too low, producing a slow loop with no speed.
Racket tip pointing at the ground at contact, forcing a low hit with no forward swing.
No slice (low-to-high), or chopping too steeply so the ball pops up.
Serving too hard: most go out, forcing a soft second serve.
Serving flat with no slice, so it jumps off the glass and is easy to attack.
Watching it fly then starting to run, so you never get set at the net.
Being predictable: same direction, length and pace every time.

phases.preparation: Stand ~1.5-2m from the centre line, racket up high above the ball, weight ready to move forward; front foot forward or step in as you hit.
phases.backswing: Drop/lightly push the ball from ~shoulder height, racket above with an open face, non-hitting hand in tension.
phases.contact: Strike at the waistline (legal max), face open ~45 degrees, brushing high-to-low to slice; place via contact point and push forward for length.
phases.follow_through: Drive weight forward and use momentum to move straight into the net with consistent steps, then split ready for the first volley.

shots.purpose: The return's job is not to win the point but to neutralise the server's attack with a deep, low ball so you survive the first volley and work your way to the net.
cues: Set a pre-serve routine and split-step as the server strikes so you are balanced, not flat-footed.
First serve: return low with slice. Second/weak serve: lob deep and move up to take the net.
Prioritise how tight and low the ball stays over the net rather than how fast you hit it.
Default to returning at whoever served, since they must hit the first volley while still moving.
When the serve is too good, go down the line (technically easier, pushes onto their partner).
Prepare the racket early, before the ball reaches the glass, and stay low through contact.
On a ball into the glass, read the panel: if it reaches the first panel move off and let it come out; if only the second, take it early.
On a body serve, move quickly off the line to hit a clean forehand or backhand — don't play it off the glass.
Get low, push, and don't stand up until the shot is finished so the ball stays down.
The instant you hit, recover your position — don't stand and watch.
mistakes: Trying to hit a winner off the return — it sails out and gives free points.
Attempting a lob on a strong serve, producing a short lob that gets smashed.
Hitting too hard when a tight low ball would neutralise better.
Returning high or lifting the ball, letting the opponent destroy the first volley.
Playing off the glass unnecessarily, especially on a heavy-slice serve that dies on the wall.
On a good serve, returning to the net player's forehand instead of chest/backhand.
Rushing and hitting too soon — the return needs timing and a relaxed read.
Failing to recover after the return, leaving a big open space.

phases.preparation: Ready position with a repeatable pre-serve routine, split-step as the server contacts, weight forward and alert.
phases.backswing: Short compact take-back prepared early — racket set before the ball reaches the glass, no last-second wind-up.
phases.contact: Early and in front, low with knees bent and body down, slice for control; stop before hitting if you had to run for it.
phases.follow_through: Controlled finish keeping the ball low and tight over the net, then move up toward the net and recover immediately.
